info.topictitle: 2.3.1 Demand and supply curves forming a market equilibrium

markdown:
<definition term="Market Equilibrium">When quantity demanded is **equal **to quantity supplied, and there is** no tendency** for the price to change.</definition>

On a graph, this point is called the** equilibrium point** and it determines the **equilibrium price** and **equilibrium quantity** (see Figure 1 below).

![Figure 1: surplus (excess supply) and shortage (excess demand) in a market](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/33a60f301a05b95b5e721ca998ed1533bfddc004-2338x1813.png)

Figure 1 showcases the market for $Pencils$, with **consumers **having a **demand **shown by curve $D$ and **producers** having a **supply** shown by curve $S$ on the diagram below.

Consider the market at price $P_1$. Here the quantity demanded is $Q_1$ and the quantity supplied is $Q_2$.

1. At this high price, the **quantity demanded** of the good is **less** than the **quantity supplied**.
2. Therefore there is **excess supply** in the market, or **surplus**.

<definition term="Surplus">The extra supply that results when quantity supplied is greater than quantity demanded.</definition>

<callout type="warning">Students often think surplus is caused by "overproduction."

While true in part, the real issue is that the price is too high, discouraging consumers from purchasing the product.</callout>

Similarly, consider $P_2$. Here the quantity demanded is $Q_2$ and the quantity supplied is $Q_1$.

* At this low price, the **quantity demanded** of the good is **more** than the **quantity supplied.**
* Therefore there is **excess demand** in the market, or **shortage**.

<definition term="Shortage">The excess of quantity demanded over quantity supplied.</definition>

### Excess supply and excess demand lead to market equilibrium


<definition term="Market Equilibrium">When quantity demanded is **equal **to quantity supplied, and there is** no tendency** for the price to change.</definition>

![Figure 2: surplus and shortage lead to market equilibrium](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c42ee31221f0d0cc49d809b642c3d49f863bee66-2338x1813.png)

**When there is** **excess supply, there is a downward pressure on the price:**

1. Quantity supplied is **higher** than quantity demanded, $Q_2>Q_1$.
2. Producers** cannot sell all** of their supply and thus will lower prices to **encourage consumers to buy their good or service**.
3. Therefore, as long as there is **excess supply**, there will be a **downward pressure** on the price until it reaches $P_e$ and the **market is cleared**.

**When there is** **excess demand, there is an upward pressure on the price:**

1. Quantity supplied is **lower** than quantity demanded, $Q_1 < Q_2$.
2. Producers **quickly sell** their supply and realize more people still demanding  their good or service.
3. Thus they will **increase the price** so they can produce more and **benefit off the high demand**.
4. The higher price will start to** reduce **some of the quantity demanded.
5. This will keep happening as long as there is **excess demand **and through the **upward pressure** on price till $P_e$.

Hence, whenever there is an **excess supply** or** excess demand:**

1. The **prices will change** until the **equilibrium price** $P_e$ is reached.
2. At the **equilibrium price** $P_e$, there is an equilibrium in the **quantity supplied and demanded**. This quantity is referred to as the **equilibrium quantity **$Q_e$.
3. This point is the **market equilibrium**: the forces of **supply **and **demand **are balanced since there is no price pressures. 
4. Any other pricing will lead to a **disequilibrium** in the market.
